items.q: Why do economists generally argue against protectionism?
items.correct: Tariffs and quotas raise prices for consumers, protect inefficient industries, invite retaliation, and reduce overall welfare — even when they save specific jobs, the cost per job saved typically exceeds the wage.
items.misconception: Protectionism always makes a country richer by keeping money at home.
items.random_wrong: Protectionism means protecting national parks from development.
items.why: Trade is positive-sum in standard theory — countries gain from specialisation. Students assume tariffs trap wealth domestically, missing that consumers pay higher prices and exporters lose from retaliation.

items.q: What is the principle of comparative advantage?
items.correct: Countries should specialise in producing goods with lower opportunity cost relative to others — even a country worse at everything still has a comparative advantage in something.
items.misconception: A country should produce whatever it can make best.
items.random_wrong: Comparative advantage means richer countries should help poorer ones.
items.why: Students confuse absolute and comparative advantage. The Ricardian insight is that mutually beneficial trade is possible even when one country is absolutely better at everything — based on opportunity cost.

items.q: Is a trade deficit necessarily bad?
items.correct: Not necessarily — it can reflect strong domestic demand, attractive investment opportunities, or borrowing to fund growth. Context matters more than the headline figure.
items.misconception: A trade deficit always means a country is losing wealth and failing economically.
items.random_wrong: A trade deficit means a country has no factories.
items.why: The US has run deficits for decades while growing. Trade deficits are balanced by capital inflows. Students treat deficits as automatic failure, missing the accounting identity and what it implies.

items.q: A tariff is best described as:
items.why: Correct answer: A tax on imports. “A subsidy to imports” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Selling exports below cost or below home price
items.why: It's Selling exports below cost or below home price. “Free trade agreements” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 1995. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
